verses.flow: Paul, a bondservant of God and an apostle of Jesus Christ, according to the faith of the elect of God and the knowledge of truth that is according to godliness,
verses.note: GREEK: "Bondservant" (doulos, G1401) denotes one wholly owned by a master—Paul claims the highest title of OT servants of YHWH (cf. Moses in Josh 1:2, David in Ps 89:3). "Knowledge" (epignōsis, G1922) is not mere gnōsis but a full, precise, experiential knowing. THEOLOGICAL: Paul grounds his apostleship in two coordinates: the faith of God's elect and the full knowledge of truth—linking election and revelation as inseparable realities.

verses.flow: upon the hope of eternal life, which the unlying God promised before eternal ages,
verses.note: GREEK: "Unlying" (apseudēs, G893) is a NT hapax legomenon, found only here. It intensifies God's truthfulness beyond merely "faithful"—He is by nature incapable of falsehood. "Before eternal ages" (pro chronōn aiōniōn) points to a pretemporal divine decree. THEOLOGICAL: The promise of eternal life originates in God's unchangeable nature before time itself, grounding salvation in the immutable counsel of God (cf. 2 Tim 1:9; Eph 1:4).

verses.flow: but manifested in its own appointed times His word, through the proclamation with which I was entrusted according to the command of God our Savior—
verses.note: GREEK: "Manifested" (ephanerósen, G5319) marks the contrast between the hidden eternal purpose and its historical unveiling. "Proclamation" (kērygma, G2782) denotes the authoritative herald's announcement, not casual speech. TYPOLOGY: The pattern of hidden-then-revealed echoes the mystery motif in Paul (Rom 16:25-26; Col 1:26), wherein God's eternal plan is disclosed through appointed messengers at the appointed time.

verses.flow: To Titus, a true child according to a common faith: grace and peace from God the Father and Christ Jesus our Savior.

words.m: verb present active participle accusative plural neuter

verses.flow: For this cause I left you in Crete, that you might set in order the things remaining and appoint elders in every city, as I myself directed you—
verses.note: HISTORICAL: Crete was a large Mediterranean island with numerous cities; its reputation for moral disorder (v. 12) made organized church leadership urgent. "Set in order" (epidiorthōsē, G1930) is a NT hapax, a compound verb suggesting correcting what remains deficient. STRUCTURE: This verse establishes the entire occasion and purpose of the letter—ecclesial order through qualified leadership.

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: if anyone is above reproach, a husband of one wife, having children who are faithful, not under accusation of reckless living or unruly.
For it is necessary that the overseer be above reproach, as a steward of God, not self-willed, not quick-tempered, not given to wine, not a striker, not greedy of shameful gain,
verses.note: GREEK: "Overseer" (episkopos, G1985) is used interchangeably with "elder" (presbyteros, v. 5), confirming that in the apostolic period these titles described the same office, not a hierarchical distinction. "Steward of God" (theou oikonomon) frames the overseer not as an autonomous ruler but as a household manager under divine authority. DIVINE_COUNCIL: The overseer functions as God's appointed representative in the local assembly, mirroring the stewardship pattern of delegated authority found throughout Scripture (cf. Luke 12:42; 1 Cor 4:1-2).

verses.flow: but hospitable, a lover of good, sound-minded, righteous, holy, self-controlled,

words.m: verb present middle participle accusative singular masculine
verb present active participle dative singular feminine
verb present active participle accusative plural masculine

verses.flow: holding fast the faithful word that is according to the teaching, so that he may be able both to exhort in the sound doctrine and to convict those who speak against it.
verses.note: GREEK: "Holding fast" (antechomenon, G472) conveys a tenacious clinging to, as against opposition. "Sound" (hygiainousē, G5198) is a medical metaphor—literally "healthy" doctrine, implying that false teaching is a spiritual disease. THEOLOGICAL: The elder's dual task of positive exhortation and negative refutation presupposes a fixed doctrinal standard—the "faithful word according to the teaching"—against which all claims must be measured.

verses.flow: For there are many who are both unruly, vain talkers and deceivers of minds, especially those of the circumcision,

words.m: verb present active participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: whose mouths it is necessary to stop, who overturn whole households, teaching things which they ought not, for the sake of shameful gain.
One of them, a prophet of their own, said: "Cretans are always liars, evil beasts, idle bellies."
verses.note: HISTORICAL: Paul quotes the Cretan poet-philosopher Epimenides of Knossos (ca. 6th century BC). Calling him "a prophet of their own" employs prophētēs in its broad Greek sense of an inspired spokesman, not affirming him as a biblical prophet. The paradox of a Cretan calling all Cretans liars was famous in Greek philosophical discourse (the "Epimenides paradox").

verses.flow: This testimony is true. For which cause rebuke them sharply, that they may be sound in the faith,

words.m: verb present active participle nominative plural masculine
verb present passive participle genitive plural masculine

verses.flow: not giving heed to Jewish myths and commandments of men who are turning away from the truth.

words.m: verb perfect passive participle dative plural masculine

verses.flow: All things are clean to the clean; but to those who are defiled and unbelieving, nothing is clean, but both their mind and their conscience have been defiled.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Paul counters proto-ascetic or Judaizing purity regulations by affirming the inherent cleanness of creation for the pure in heart (cf. Mark 7:15-19; Rom 14:14, 20). The defilement is not in external things but in the corrupted mind (nous) and conscience (syneidēsis) of the unbelieving. TYPOLOGY: This echoes Christ's teaching that defilement proceeds from within (Mark 7:20-23), fulfilling the new covenant promise of cleansed hearts (Ezek 36:25-27).

words.m: verb present active participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: They profess to know God, but by their works they deny Him, being abominable and disobedient and unto every good work worthless.
verses.note: GREEK: "Worthless" (adokimoi, G96) means "failing the test, disqualified"—used of metals that fail assaying. "Abominable" (bdelyktoi, G947) is a strong LXX term associated with idolatrous defilement (cf. bdelygma in Dan 9:27; Luke 16:15). THEOLOGICAL: The chapter closes with the sharpest indictment: a profession of God contradicted by works constitutes a functional denial of Him, rendering one reprobate concerning every good work—a warning that orthodoxy without orthopraxy is empty.

words.m: verb present active participle dative singular feminine

verses.flow: But you, speak the things which are fitting for sound doctrine:
verses.note: GREEK: "Ὑγιαινούσῃ" (hygiainousē, Strong's G5198) literally means "being healthy/sound" — a medical metaphor applied to doctrine. Paul uses this term repeatedly in the Pastoral Epistles (1 Tim 1:10; 2 Tim 4:3) to distinguish apostolic teaching from the diseased teachings of false instructors.

words.m: verb present active participle accusative plural masculine

verses.flow: That aged men be sober-minded, dignified, self-controlled, being sound in the faith, in love, in endurance;

words.m: verb perfect passive participle accusative plural feminine

verses.flow: Aged women likewise, in deportment reverent, not slanderers, not enslaved to much wine, teachers of what is good,
verses.note: GREEK: "Ἱεροπρεπεῖς" (hieroprepeis, G2412) is a hapax legomenon in the NT, meaning "befitting a sacred person" or "reverent as befits a temple." The compound of hieros (sacred) and prepō (fitting) evokes priestly comportment. "Καλοδιδασκάλους" (kalodidaskalous, G2567) is also a NT hapax, a compound meaning "teachers of what is beautiful/good."

verses.flow: that they might train the young women to be lovers of their husbands, lovers of their children,

words.m: verb present passive participle accusative plural feminine

verses.flow: self-controlled, pure, workers at home, good, being subject to their own husbands, that the word of God be not blasphemed.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The purpose clause "that the word of God be not blasphemed" (ἵνα μὴ ὁ λόγος τοῦ θεοῦ βλασφημῆται) reveals that ethical conduct among believers carries a missional dimension. Dishonorable living causes outsiders to speak evil against God's word itself. This echoes the OT concern that Israel's conduct among the nations either honored or profaned God's name (Isa 52:5; Ezek 36:20-23).

verses.flow: The younger men likewise exhort to be self-controlled,

words.m: verb present middle participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: in all things showing yourself a pattern of good works, in your teaching showing incorruptness, dignity,

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: sound speech beyond condemnation, that the one who is of the opposition may be put to shame, having nothing evil to say concerning us.

words.m: verb present active participle accusative plural masculine

verses.flow: Bondservants are to be subject to their own masters in all things, to be well-pleasing, not contradicting,

words.m: verb present middle participle accusative plural masculine
verb present middle participle accusative plural masculine

verses.flow: not pilfering, but showing all good faithfulness, that they may adorn the doctrine of God our Savior in all things.
verses.note: GREEK: "Κοσμῶσιν" (kosmōsin, G2885) means "to adorn, arrange, make beautiful" — from which English "cosmetic" derives. The striking image is that faithful living "adorns" or "beautifies" the doctrine of God. "Νοσφιζομένους" (nosphizomenous, G3557) means "to pilfer, embezzle, keep back for oneself" — the same verb used of Ananias and Sapphira in Acts 5:2-3.

verses.flow: For the grace of God has appeared, bringing salvation to all men,
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "Ἐπεφάνη" (epephanē, G2014) is an aorist passive of epiphainō, meaning "appeared, was manifested." This term carried imperial overtones in the Greco-Roman world, used for the visible manifestation of a deity or the official arrival of an emperor. Paul subverts this by declaring that God's saving grace — not Caesar — has made its divine epiphany to all humanity. TYPOLOGY: The appearance of grace echoes the Shekinah theology of God's visible glory manifesting among his people (Exod 40:34-35; Isa 60:1-2).

words.m: verb present active participle nominative singular feminine
verb aorist middle participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: training us, that having denied ungodliness and worldly desires, we might live self-controlledly and righteously and godly in the present age,

words.m: verb present middle participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: awaiting the blessed hope and appearing of the glory of our great God and Savior, Jesus Christ,
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: This verse is among the strongest affirmations of Christ's deity in the NT. The Greek construction "τοῦ μεγάλου θεοῦ καὶ σωτῆρος ἡμῶν Ἰησοῦ Χριστοῦ" follows the Granville Sharp rule: a single article governs both "God" and "Savior," identifying them as one referent — Jesus Christ is here called "our great God and Savior." GREEK: "Ἐπιφάνειαν" (epiphaneian, G2015) refers to Christ's second appearing in glory, forming a deliberate parallel with the first epiphany of grace in v.11. The "blessed hope" (μακαρίαν ἐλπίδα) is not mere optimism but the assured expectation of eschatological consummation.

verses.flow: who gave himself on behalf of us, that he might redeem us from all lawlessness and purify for himself a people for his own possession, zealous of good works.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: "Λυτρώσηται" (lytrōsētai, G3084) means "to redeem, ransom" — evoking the Exodus redemption where God purchased Israel from bondage (Exod 6:6; Deut 7:8). "Λαὸν περιούσιον" (laon periousion) directly echoes the LXX of Exodus 19:5 and Deuteronomy 14:2, where Israel is called God's "treasured/peculiar possession." Christ now constitutes for himself a new covenant people through his self-offering, fulfilling the typological pattern of Israel's redemption. GREEK: "Ζηλωτὴν" (zēlōtēn, G2207) means "one who is zealous," carrying connotations of passionate devotion — this redeemed people is characterized not by passive reception but by active zeal for good works.

verses.flow: These things speak and exhort and rebuke with all authority. Let no one despise you.
verses.note: GREEK: "Ἐπιταγῆς" (epitagēs, G2003) denotes divinely delegated authority or command — this is not personal assertiveness but apostolic commissioning. "Περιφρονείτω" (periphroneitō, G4065) is a NT hapax legomenon meaning "to think around, think beyond, despise, disregard" — stronger than the more common kataphroneō, suggesting an active looking past or dismissal of Titus's authority. Compare Paul's similar charge to Timothy (1 Tim 4:12).

verses.flow: Remind them to be subject to rulers and authorities, to be obedient, to be ready for every good work,

words.m: verb present middle participle accusative plural masculine

verses.flow: to blaspheme no one, to be peaceable, gentle, showing all meekness toward all men.

words.m: verb present passive participle nominative plural masculine
verb present active participle nominative plural masculine
verb present active participle nominative plural masculine
verb present active participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: For we ourselves also were once foolish, disobedient, being led astray, serving various desires and pleasures, living in malice and envy, hateful, hating one another.
But when the kindness and the love-toward-man of God our Savior appeared,
verses.note: GREEK: "chrēstotēs" (χρηστότης, Strong's G5544) denotes goodness or kindness in active benevolence. "Philanthrōpia" (φιλανθρωπία, G5363) literally means "love toward mankind" — a rare NT term (also Acts 28:2), here ascribed to God himself. "Epephanē" (ἐπεφάνη) is an aorist passive of "epiphainō," meaning "appeared" or "was manifested," the same root as "epiphany," carrying theophanic weight — the divine breaking into the visible realm.

verses.flow: not out of works which are in righteousness, which we did, but according to his own mercy he saved us, through the washing of regeneration and renewal of the Holy Spirit,
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: This verse is among the most concentrated soteriological statements in the NT. Salvation is explicitly grounded not in works of righteousness performed by humans but in God's mercy alone. "Palingenesia" (παλιγγενεσία, G3824) is a rare term occurring only here and Matt 19:28 in the NT; it means "regeneration" or "new birth" — here describing the individual believer's renewal, whereas in Matthew it refers to the cosmic renewal. The coupling of "washing" (loutrón) with "regeneration" and "renewal of the Holy Spirit" links baptismal imagery with pneumatological transformation (cf. John 3:5, Ezek 36:25-27).

verses.flow: whom he poured out upon us richly through Jesus Christ our Savior,
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The pouring out (ekchéō) of the Spirit echoes Joel 2:28-29 (LXX 3:1-2), fulfilled at Pentecost (Acts 2:17-18, 33). The mediatorial role of Jesus Christ as the one "through whom" the Spirit is poured connects to his ascension and enthronement as Lord. Note that God the Father is called "Savior" in v4 and Jesus Christ is called "Savior" here in v6, affirming a shared divine identity in the work of salvation.

words.m: verb aorist passive participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: that having been justified by that one's grace, we might become heirs according to the hope of eternal life.
verses.note: GREEK: "Dikaiōthentes" (δικαιωθέντες) is an aorist passive participle — "having been justified" — indicating a completed divine action upon the believer. "Klēronomoi" (κληρονόμοι, G2818) means "heirs," drawing on the OT concept of inheritance (klēros) given to God's people (cf. Rom 8:17, Gal 3:29). The phrase "hope of eternal life" (elpída zōēs aiōniou) frames the inheritance as eschatological — already legally secured but awaiting full consummation.

words.m: verb perfect active participle nominative plural masculine

verses.flow: Faithful is the word, and concerning these things I desire you to affirm strongly, that those who have believed God might be careful to maintain good works. These things are good and profitable to men.
verses.note: STRUCTURE: "Pistos ho logos" (Faithful is the word) is one of the Pastoral Epistles' distinctive formulae (cf. 1 Tim 1:15, 3:1, 4:9; 2 Tim 2:11), marking what precedes (vv3-7) as a creedal or hymnic confession of established apostolic teaching. Paul charges Titus to "affirm strongly" (diabebaioûsthai, G1226, a rare intensified compound) so that right doctrine produces right practice — "good works" (kalōn ergōn) being the fruit, never the root, of salvation.

verses.flow: But foolish questionings and genealogies and strifes and fights about the law avoid, for they are unprofitable and vain.
A factious man after a first and second admonition refuse,
verses.note: GREEK: "Hairetikon" (αἱρετικόν, G141) is a hapax legomenon in the NT. It derives from "hairesis" (a choosing, faction, sect) and denotes one who causes division by promoting factious opinions. This is the origin of the English word "heretic," though in context it emphasizes the divisive social behavior as much as doctrinal error. The measured process — a first and second warning before rejection — reflects the principle of ecclesial discipline seen also in Matt 18:15-17.

words.m: verb perfect active participle nominative singular masculine
verb present active participle nominative singular masculine

verses.flow: knowing that such a one has been perverted and sins, being self-condemned.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "Exestraptai" (ἐξέστραπται, perfect passive of ekstrephō) means "has been turned inside out" or "perverted" — the perfect tense indicating a settled, corrupted state. "Autokatakritos" (αὐτοκατάκριτος, G843) is another hapax legomenon meaning "self-condemned" — the factious person's own persistence after warning serves as the evidence of their condemnation, requiring no external judicial verdict.

verses.flow: When I send Artemas to you, or Tychicus, be diligent to come to me at Nicopolis, for there I have determined to winter.
Zenas the lawyer and Apollos send forward diligently, that nothing may be lacking to them.
And let our own also learn to maintain good works for necessary needs, that they may not be unfruitful.

words.m: verb present active participle accusative plural masculine

verses.flow: All those with me greet you. Greet those who love us in the faith. Grace be with you all.
